questions.question: What is the main structural reform announced in the Brussels regional government's coalition agreement to reduce operational costs?
options: Transfer of all regional responsibilities to the federal government
Merger of 25 regional structures into 4 entities with expected cost reductions
Elimination of all regional civil service positions through layoffs
Complete privatization of all regional administrative services
questions.explanation: The coalition agreement explicitly commits to merging 25 regional structures into 4 pillars (entities), with a target of 20-30% reduction in operational costs. This administrative reform is a key mechanism to achieve the balanced budget goal by 2029.
questions.sourceTitle: Regional budget: targeting balance by 2029

questions.question: Which of the following tax measures is NOT included in the 2026 coalition agreement?
options: Introduction of a City Tax on non-residents
Commitment to avoid a kilometre-based road charge
Raising the exemption threshold for registration duties
Reduction of regional personal income tax by one point
questions.explanation: The coalition agreement explicitly states 'No new taxes — no City Tax, no kilometre charge,' ruling out both a City Tax and a kilometre charge. The other options reflect actual commitments: a one-point income tax reduction and a raised registration duty threshold for property transactions.
questions.sourceTitle: Regional budget: targeting balance by 2029

questions.question: What new institutional body was created by the Brussels Parliament in January 2026 to address public cleanliness issues?
options: An executive task force composed solely of municipal mayors
A deliberative committee combining randomly selected citizens and elected representatives
A technical department within the Brussels Cleanliness Agency
An inter-regional coordination board with Flanders and Wallonia
questions.explanation: The Brussels Parliament established a 7th deliberative committee on public cleanliness comprising 45 randomly selected citizens and 15 MPs to formulate recommendations on waste management. This innovative democratic structure represents a new approach to citizen participation in governance.
questions.sourceTitle: Cleanliness: regional brigades and immediate fine collection

questions.question: According to the Regional Policy Declaration, which infrastructure investment is planned to combat illegal dumping in high-density neighbourhoods?
options: Underground containers and compactors, along with smart cameras in at-risk areas
Mobile collection trucks with GPS tracking systems
Above-ground sorting facilities managed by private contractors
Temporary fencing barriers around waste disposal sites
questions.explanation: The RPD commits to deploying underground containers and compactors in high-density neighbourhoods and installing smart cameras to monitor areas prone to illegal dumping. This dual approach combines preventive infrastructure with technological enforcement to address the cleanliness challenges identified by citizens.
questions.sourceTitle: Cleanliness: regional brigades and immediate fine collection

questions.question: What change did the Brussels government make to the Renolution program in its February 2026 coalition agreement?
options: Renolution was expanded with increased funding for energy renovation projects
Renolution was abolished entirely without any replacement mechanism
Renolution was merged with the LEZ to create a unified environmental program
Renolution was replaced by a zero-interest loans program for energy renovation
questions.explanation: The coalition agreement of February 2026 replaced Renolution with zero-interest loans, though experts note this change creates ambiguous signals for the climate trajectory since it lacks a dedicated energy transition budget.
questions.sourceTitle: Climate: LEZ maintained, Renolution replaced, targets pending

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Brussels government's appeal against the October 2025 green zones construction freeze ruling?
options: To protect specific brownfield sites named in the coalition agreement
To eliminate all environmental protections for green spaces in the region
To pursue legal clarity regarding the broader legal framework for construction in green zones
To align Brussels regulations with neighboring regions' construction policies
questions.explanation: The government filed an appeal in March 2026 citing 'legal clarity' as the stated reason, though the appeal concerns a broader legal framework than the specific brownfield sites protected in the coalition agreement, which has drawn criticism from environmental associations.
questions.sourceTitle: Climate: LEZ maintained, Renolution replaced, targets pending

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the three key agencies responsible for implementing Brussels' digital governance commitments?
options: Easy Brussels, the administrative simplification agency
BeCentral, the data centre operator
IRISbox, the online services platform
Paradigm (CIRB), the regional IT centre
questions.explanation: BeCentral is a partner organisation for attracting technology companies to Brussels as part of the 'AI capital' strategy, not a key implementing agency. The three key agencies are Paradigm (CIRB), Easy Brussels, and IRISbox, which directly deliver digital services and administrative simplification.
questions.sourceTitle: Digital: bridging the digital divide and administrative simplification

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the 'Only once' principle mentioned in Brussels' Digital Regional Policy Declaration?
options: To stop asking citizens for data that the administration already holds
To reduce the number of digital platforms available to the public
To allow each citizen only one digital account across all services
To limit citizens to one visit to municipal offices per year
questions.explanation: The 'Only once' principle is a core administrative simplification measure designed to avoid redundant data collection and improve the citizen experience. It reflects a commitment to using information the administration already possesses rather than repeatedly requesting the same data from citizens.
questions.sourceTitle: Digital: bridging the digital divide and administrative simplification

questions.question: What are the three main levers the Brussels government plans to use for the Urban Free Zone at the Port and Audi Forest?
options: Environmental certifications, tourist attractions, and business partnerships
Housing subsidies, transport infrastructure, and educational programs
Real estate development, cultural events, and international trade agreements
Tax reduction, administrative fast-track, and dedicated employment support
questions.explanation: The Urban Free Zone strategy relies on a tax lever (property tax and registration rights reduction), an administrative lever (single Fast-Track window), and a human lever (dedicated Actiris cell for jobs). This three-pronged approach aims to attract businesses and counter the construction sector crisis.
questions.sourceTitle: Economy: Urban Free Zone (Port + Audi Forest), AI capital

questions.question: How did the federal government's hotel VAT change in March 2026 impact Brussels?
options: It introduced a new tax on restaurant meals across all of Belgium
It increased the VAT on hotel stays from 6% to 12%, affecting Brussels's tourism and business sectors
It decreased the VAT on hotel stays to encourage more visitors to the region
It created tax exemptions for international business travelers staying in Brussels
questions.explanation: Effective 1 March 2026, the federal government raised hotel VAT from 6% to 12%, directly impacting Brussels, which accounts for the majority of Belgium's tourist and business overnight stays. This was partially offset by a VAT reduction on soft drinks in hospitality establishments.
questions.sourceTitle: Economy: Urban Free Zone (Port + Audi Forest), AI capital

questions.question: Which institution will take over full management of Dutch-speaking out-of-school care in Brussels starting September 2026?
options: A new independent agency created by the Region
The French Community (FWB)
The Brussels Region directly
The Flemish Community (VGC)
questions.explanation: The VGC will assume complete responsibility for Dutch-speaking out-of-school care management, including policy, accreditation, budgets and coordination. This transition concludes a five-year phase that began when the BOA decree came into force in 2021.
questions.sourceTitle: Education: childcare places, school dropout and Cocof/VGC tensions

questions.question: In Brussels education governance, what role does Cocof primarily exercise?
options: It oversees both French and Dutch-language schools equally
It provides only vocational training to allophone jobseekers
It handles transferred competences in French-speaking continuing education and lifelong learning
It manages all education in Brussels through the regional government
questions.explanation: Cocof exercises transferred competences specific to French-speaking education, particularly in continuing education and lifelong learning domains. Education in Brussels is primarily under the Communities (FWB and Flemish Community), with the Region intervening through specialized bodies like Cocof and VGC.
questions.sourceTitle: Education: childcare places, school dropout and Cocof/VGC tensions

questions.question: What is the Regional Policy Declaration's main employment objective for Brussels by the end of the current parliamentary term?
options: Achieve a 70% employment rate
Reduce unemployment to match the Flemish level
Increase bilingual speakers by 50%
Create 10,000 new jobs in industrial sectors
questions.explanation: The Regional Policy Declaration of February 2026 sets an ambitious 70% employment rate target for Brussels. This represents a significant challenge given the current structural employment rate of approximately 55% documented by the Itinera Institute study, revealing the scale of Brussels' labour market gap compared to other Belgian regions.
questions.sourceTitle: Employment: 70% target, enhanced activation and bilingualism

questions.question: According to the Itinera Institute study, what unique negative employment trend distinguishes Brussels from Flanders and Wallonia between 2006 and 2022?
options: Employment in the north improved while the south deteriorated
Both women's employment stagnated and men's employment worsened
Female employment grew but male employment declined significantly
Youth employment increased while overall employment decreased
questions.explanation: The Itinera study reveals that Brussels is the only region where women's employment made zero progress and men's employment actually worsened during this period. In contrast, both Flanders and Wallonia saw employment gains across gender groups, highlighting Brussels' distinctive structural challenge in the labour market.
questions.sourceTitle: Employment: 70% target, enhanced activation and bilingualism

questions.question: According to the February 2026 Coalition Agreement, what is replacing the Renolution premium system starting in 2027?
options: Direct grants to households for energy renovation
Mandatory renovation requirements without financial support
Zero-interest loans with an annual envelope
Tax deductions for energy-efficient upgrades
questions.explanation: The Coalition Agreement replaces Renolution premiums with zero-interest loans from 2027 onwards, with an envelope of EUR 66 million per year. This shift is designed to unlock housing financing but has raised concerns about accessibility for lower-income households, as noted by tax law experts.
questions.sourceTitle: Housing: EUR 400M for the SLRB, 1,000 public housing units

questions.question: What is the stated deadline for all Brussels homes to reach a minimum EPC energy label E standard?
questions.explanation: Brussels regulations require all homes to reach at least EPC label E by 2033. This deadline is significant because it creates pressure on renovation timelines, particularly in the context of the transition from premium support to zero-interest loans, which some industry experts fear may delay renovations.
questions.sourceTitle: Housing: EUR 400M for the SLRB, 1,000 public housing units

questions.question: In the Brussels-Capital Region's new institutional structure, which two bodies now oversee employment and vocational training separately?
options: The Ministry of Economy and the Ministry of Education
Actiris and Bruxelles Formation
Cocof and the Regional Parliament
The Brussels Employment Agency and Regional Training Institute
questions.explanation: The institutional reform places Actiris (employment) under one minister and Bruxelles Formation (vocational training) under another, a split that has drawn criticism from opposition parties who cite international recommendations for integration of these functions.
questions.sourceTitle: Institutional reform: merging 25 structures into 4 pillars

questions.question: What is the main concern raised about institutional bilingualism in the Brussels-Capital Region's recent governance changes?
options: English has replaced French as the primary language of government
Citizens are no longer required to speak both official languages
The Dutch-language version of policy documents was delayed, and Dutch language proficiency among residents has declined significantly over 25 years
The government refuses to fund language training programs
questions.explanation: The delay in publishing the Dutch-language Regional Policy Declaration highlighted ongoing bilingualism challenges, particularly relevant given that Dutch proficiency has fallen from one-third to under one-quarter of the population since 2000, reflecting a structural language shift in Brussels.
questions.sourceTitle: Institutional reform: merging 25 structures into 4 pillars

questions.question: According to the 2026 Coalition Agreement, what will replace the Metro 3 project as the main public transport investment?
options: A new bus rapid transit system connecting peripheral districts
Additional STIB construction sites in the city centre
A tram completing the loop of the existing network
An extension of the suburban rail network to Bordet
questions.explanation: The agreement freezes Metro 3 for 10 years and redirects investment towards a tram line, marking a significant shift in Brussels' mobility strategy. This decision ends the original Metro 3 extension project and prioritizes completing the existing tram network loop instead.
questions.sourceTitle: Mobility: new regional plan, Metro 3 frozen, LEZ maintained

questions.question: What is the guiding principle for Brussels mobility policy according to the new Regional Development Plan (DPR)?
options: Investment should focus equally on all transport modes regardless of safety
A hierarchy prioritizing pedestrians, cyclists, public transport, and cars in that order
Cars and public transport have equal priority in urban planning
Cyclists and cars share the same priority level above public transport
questions.explanation: The STOP principle establishes a clear hierarchy where pedestrians come first, followed by cyclists, then public transport, and finally cars. This reflects a fundamental reorientation of Brussels' mobility governance toward more sustainable and human-centered transport planning.
questions.sourceTitle: Mobility: new regional plan, Metro 3 frozen, LEZ maintained

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Regional Security Council (CORES) in Brussels?
options: To investigate individual crime cases reported by citizens
To manage the budget for all police forces in Belgium
To coordinate security responses between Brussels mayors, police zone chiefs, and federal authorities
To set criminal sentences for offenders across the Brussels region
questions.explanation: CORES brings together the 19 Brussels mayors, 6 police zone chiefs, the King's Prosecutor and OCAM representatives to coordinate regional security. The council was activated in March 2026 following international security concerns, demonstrating its role as a coordination mechanism rather than an investigative or judicial body.
questions.sourceTitle: Security: anti-drug commissioner, €10M for stations, stable crime rate

questions.question: According to the Regional Policy Declaration, which new authority is being created to address drug-related crime in Brussels?
options: A federal anti-drug task force with headquarters in Antwerp
An international border control agency for drug trafficking
A municipal enforcement unit in each of the 19 Brussels communes
A regional anti-drug commissioner with an integrated regional drug plan
questions.explanation: The RPD establishes a regional anti-drug commissioner and an integrated regional drug plan using a chain approach covering prevention, harm reduction, and enforcement. This mirrors the federal mechanism and responds to the significant rise in drug-related offences noted in the 2024 crime data.
questions.sourceTitle: Security: anti-drug commissioner, €10M for stations, stable crime rate

questions.question: According to the Coalition Agreement of February 2026, what is the primary purpose of the EUR 10 million allocated to Brussels stations?
options: To develop commercial spaces and improve passenger amenities
To fund a new regional drug commissioner office
To enhance security through cameras, police presence, cleaning, and support for vulnerable people
To renovate train platforms and modernize station infrastructure
questions.explanation: The Coalition Agreement specifically dedicates EUR 10 million for security measures at the Midi and Nord stations, including cameras, police, cleaning services, and support for vulnerable people. This reflects the region's commitment to social security integration at major transport hubs.
questions.sourceTitle: Social affairs: EUR 10M for stations, drug commissioner, services protected

questions.question: What is 'Health in All Policies' (HIAP) as outlined in the Democratic Programme for the Region (DPR)?
options: A federal program that coordinates healthcare delivery between different Belgian regions
A specialized healthcare facility focused on preventive medicine and health screening
The integration of health considerations across all regional policy areas such as urban planning, mobility, and housing
A requirement for all Brussels hospitals to adopt standardized treatment protocols
questions.explanation: HIAP is a cross-cutting approach that embeds health dimensions into broader regional policies beyond traditional healthcare. It recognizes that factors like urban design, transportation systems, and housing quality significantly impact public health outcomes.
questions.sourceTitle: Social affairs: EUR 10M for stations, drug commissioner, services protected

questions.question: What is the main objective of the 'Planning Amnesty' reform included in the Regional Policy Declaration?
options: To speed up the approval process for all new construction projects
To transfer responsibility for permits from urban.brussels to the Royal Commission
To eliminate all heritage protection requirements in Brussels
To regularise historic planning violations and reduce the backlog of infringement cases
questions.explanation: The planning amnesty creates a compliance mechanism for situations predating a cut-off date, aiming to reduce infringement backlogs and provide legal certainty to property owners. This is distinct from speeding up future permits or changing heritage protections.
questions.sourceTitle: Urban Planning: faster permits and planning amnesty

questions.question: What structural change does the CRMS reform (Royal Commission for Monuments and Sites) introduce for renovation projects in protected areas?
options: It transfers heritage decisions to the urban.brussels digital platform
It replaces the binding opinion of the Commission with a simple advisory opinion
It creates a mandatory two-year waiting period for heritage approvals
It eliminates all heritage protection requirements
questions.explanation: The CRMS reform removes the binding nature of the Commission's opinion, replacing it with advisory input. This aims to unblock renovation projects in heritage areas while maintaining heritage protection—balancing development speed with cultural preservation.
questions.sourceTitle: Urban Planning: faster permits and planning amnesty

questions.question: According to the handistreaming ordinance of 2016, which of the following is NOT a requirement for Brussels-Capital Region policies?
options: Considering disability aspects in public procurement and subsidies
Creating a dedicated budget allocation for disability services in every government ministry
Submitting an interim report to Parliament on progress
Systematically incorporating disability into all regional policies
questions.explanation: The 2016 handistreaming ordinance requires systematic incorporation of disability across policies and parliamentary reporting, but does not mandate a dedicated budget line in every ministry. The content highlights the absence of specific budgets in the current Regional Policy Declaration as a gap, not a legal requirement.
questions.sourceTitle: Accessibility and disability in Brussels: transport, employment and data

questions.question: What is the main philosophical shift that occurred in the ACS scheme following the 2015 reform?
options: The scheme introduced mandatory university degrees for all subsidised contract workers
The scheme was completely eliminated and replaced with direct government employment in non-profits
The scheme shifted focus exclusively toward subsidising positions in local authorities rather than non-profits
The scheme changed from structural support for the social sector to an activation policy aimed at helping unemployed people transition to sustainable employment
questions.explanation: The 2015 reform marked a deliberate philosophical change in how ACS positions were understood, moving away from providing permanent jobs in the social sector toward treating positions as stepping stones for jobseekers. This shift has created tension with non-profits who argue their positions fulfill essential, permanent functions.
questions.sourceTitle: Subsidised Contract Workers: the weakened pillar of Brussels non-profit employment

questions.question: According to the content, what is the 'ACS differential' problem identified by non-profit employers?
options: Employers must pay ACS workers the same salaries as regular staff, but the regional premium does not fully cover these costs, forcing employers to absorb the difference
There is a wage gap between ACS workers and those employed in the public administration sector
The ACS scheme provides different subsidy levels depending on the size of the non-profit organization
Different regions of Belgium receive unequal amounts of ACS funding from the federal government
questions.explanation: The ACS differential reveals a structural underfunding issue: non-profit agreements establish higher salary scales than those the ACS premium recognizes, and new benefits apply without supplementary budget. This particularly weakens smaller, subsidy-dependent organizations—read more about how this affects the sector's sustainability.
questions.sourceTitle: Subsidised Contract Workers: the weakened pillar of Brussels non-profit employment

questions.question: How are citizens selected to participate in the Brussels Parliament's deliberative commissions?
options: Through a public election held across all 19 Brussels municipalities
By nomination from local community organizations and civil society groups
Through a double random draw using national register numbers from residents aged 16 and over
Through a voluntary application process open to all Brussels residents
questions.explanation: The deliberative commissions use a random selection process to ensure representative participation. This democratic mechanism is designed to include ordinary citizens regardless of their political knowledge or activism level, making it distinct from self-selected participation methods.
questions.sourceTitle: Citizens' assemblies: 6 commissions completed, 205+ recommendations, 7th in preparation

questions.question: According to research on Brussels residents' well-being, which of the following is identified as the most influential factor affecting life satisfaction?
options: Housing affordability
Employment opportunities
Physical and mental health
Access to public transportation
questions.explanation: The IBSA Focus survey based on EU-SILC 2023 data identifies health (both physical and mental) as the primary determinant of life satisfaction, followed by standard of living, social relationships, housing, and work. This finding highlights why Brussels governance should prioritize health and well-being policies.
questions.sourceTitle: Well-being and quality of life in Brussels: indicators, rankings and challenges

questions.question: Why is Belgium becoming an increasingly attractive location for major data centre investments?
options: Belgium has implemented special tax incentives exclusively for data centre operators
The Belgian government has prioritized data centres in its Regional Policy Declaration
The Brussels-Capital Region has the lowest electricity prices in Europe
Established data centre hubs in other European cities are reaching capacity and redirecting investment towards Belgium
questions.explanation: The content explains that saturation of established hubs like Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin is redirecting investment towards Belgium. This geographic shift is driving the rapid growth of the Belgian data centre sector, now accounting for twice the European average share of national electricity consumption.
questions.sourceTitle: Data centres and AI: impact on the Brussels energy system

questions.question: How is the energy demand from artificial intelligence within data centres expected to change between 2024 and 2030?
options: AI energy consumption will decrease as models become more efficient
AI will account for a stable proportion of data centre energy use throughout this period
AI's share of data centre energy consumption is projected to roughly double or triple, while the type of energy use will shift from model training to user queries
AI energy consumption will be phased out in favor of traditional computing methods
questions.explanation: The content indicates that AI's share of data centre energy consumption could grow from approximately 15% in 2024 to 35-50% by 2030. Crucially, the composition of this energy use is shifting: inference (everyday user queries) now dominates over training, meaning millions of daily user interactions increasingly drive energy demand rather than the initial model construction.
questions.sourceTitle: Data centres and AI: impact on the Brussels energy system

questions.question: Which institution has virtually no power over compulsory education decisions in Brussels, even though Brussels residents pay taxes to it?
options: The Brussels-Capital Region government
The European Commission
The Wallonia-Brussels Federation Parliament
The Flemish Community Parliament
questions.explanation: Brussels residents pay regional taxes to the Brussels-Capital Region, yet education is managed by the Communities (FWB for francophone schools, Flemish Community for Dutch-language schools). This institutional separation means the Brussels government cannot prevent education cuts decided by these Communities.
questions.sourceTitle: Compulsory education in Brussels: the impact of FWB and Flemish Community budget cuts

questions.question: In the FWB's 2026-2027 school year reforms, what change is being made to upper secondary teachers' classroom teaching requirements?
options: Their teaching hours will be eliminated in favor of administrative work
They will teach 22 hours per week instead of 20 hours
They will teach 18 hours per week instead of 20 hours
They will teach 20 hours per week instead of 22 hours
questions.explanation: Upper secondary teachers are moving from 20 to 22 hours of classroom teaching per week, aligning them with lower secondary teachers. This mechanically reduces temporary teaching positions available in Brussels schools, as fewer hours remain to distribute among staff.
questions.sourceTitle: Compulsory education in Brussels: the impact of FWB and Flemish Community budget cuts

questions.question: Which Brussels institution provides free assessment and referral services for businesses in difficulty through the Centre pour Entreprises en difficulté (CEd)?
options: The FPS Economy
Réseau Entreprendre Bruxelles
hub.brussels
The Brussels Enterprise Court
questions.explanation: hub.brussels operates the CEd, offering free assessment and referral to struggling businesses. This is one of several regional support mechanisms available, though the number of cases handled relative to total bankruptcies is not yet publicly documented.
questions.sourceTitle: Bankruptcies in Brussels: overview, sectors and regional mechanisms

questions.question: What constitutional mechanism was triggered when a conflict of interest motion was filed against the police zone merger project in the Brussels Parliament on 13 March 2026?
options: A dissolution of the current police zone structure
A 60-day consultation period between the Brussels Parliament and the federal Chamber
An immediate suspension of all police operations in Brussels
A mandatory referendum among all Brussels municipalities
questions.explanation: The conflict of interest motion triggered a 60-day consultation period, which is a constitutional mechanism that suspends the federal legislative procedure and forces inter-institutional dialogue—though it does not definitively block the project. This allows time for institutional actors to address concerns before proceeding.
questions.sourceTitle: Police zone merger: from 6 to 1, vote expected before summer 2026

questions.question: What is the main structural change in the new Regional Mobility Plan compared to Good Move?
options: All bollards will be permanently removed from Brussels streets
Public transport will be eliminated in favor of cycling infrastructure
Circulation cells will be centered on school zones instead of neighborhoods
The plan will focus only on car-free Sundays throughout the year
questions.explanation: The new plan maintains the philosophy of calmed traffic but reorganizes its basic unit: instead of neighbourhood-wide cells, it will create smaller cells focused specifically on school zones to improve road safety and quality of life around schools. This represents a fundamental shift in scale and scope from the original Good Move approach.
questions.sourceTitle: Good Move: new regional plan to succeed it

questions.question: What was the main consequence of the government transition period between June 2024 and February 2026 for Brussels mobility policy?
options: All existing neighbourhood cells were immediately dismantled and removed
All 19 communes agreed to expand the neighbourhood cell program
No new regional mobility decisions could be made, blocking expansion and investment while some communes maintained existing cells
The Brussels government completed the official evaluation of all deployed cells
questions.explanation: During this blocked period, key decisions on priority neighbourhoods, cycling infrastructure investments, regional coordination, and official evaluations were frozen. Some communes continued applying existing cells, but no new rollout could proceed, creating a governance standstill on mobility policy that lasted nearly two years.
questions.sourceTitle: Good Move: new regional plan to succeed it

questions.question: Under the February 2026 government agreement, what financial options are available to owners of non-compliant vehicles to legally drive in Brussels?
options: They can purchase an annual pass or pay a reduced fine per infraction
They must pay a one-time registration fee of 80 EUR
They must replace their vehicle within a year to avoid penalties
They can only drive during designated hours without additional payment
questions.explanation: The agreement introduced an annual pass (350 EUR, or 200 EUR at social rate) as an alternative to paying fines per infraction, which were reduced from 350 EUR to 80 EUR. This creates flexibility for vehicle owners rather than forcing them to upgrade their cars.
questions.sourceTitle: LEZ: maintained with annual pass and reduced fines

questions.question: What legal concern did the Council of State raise about reducing LEZ fines?
options: The fines are too high and burden low-income drivers unfairly
The enforcement mechanism lacks sufficient technology to track violators
The regional government lacks authority to modify fine structures
Lower fines violate the constitutional standstill principle, which protects public health achievements
questions.explanation: The Council of State argued that reducing fines represents a regression in health protection for Brussels residents, violating the standstill principle. This legal opinion references a previous Constitutional Court ruling that annulled a similar LEZ postponement, indicating real constitutional risk for the current government approach.
questions.sourceTitle: LEZ: maintained with annual pass and reduced fines

questions.question: According to the government agreement of February 2026, what replaces the frozen Metro 3 project as Brussels' major public transport investment?
options: An extension of the existing bus rapid transit network
Upgraded metro services on existing lines only
A tram completing the loop of the existing network
A light rail line connecting to neighbouring regions
questions.explanation: The agreement explicitly states that Metro 3 is replaced by a tram that completes the loop of the existing tram network. This represents a fundamental shift in investment strategy from underground metro to surface tramway infrastructure.
questions.sourceTitle: Metro 3: project frozen for 10 years, replaced by tram

questions.question: What institutional body's report on cost overruns directly triggered the decision to freeze Metro 3?
options: The Court of Audit
The Beliris cooperation agreement committee
The Brussels Parliament's special Metro 3 commission
The Council of State
questions.explanation: The Court of Audit's report revealed massive cost overruns and concluded that the project's financial sustainability was 'seriously compromised', which directly led negotiators to freeze the project during government formation talks.
questions.sourceTitle: Metro 3: project frozen for 10 years, replaced by tram

questions.question: Under the Brussels government decree of 13 July 2023, which of the following represents the maximum number of e-scooters that a single licence holder can operate?
options: 4,000 e-scooters
8,000 e-scooters
10,000 e-scooters
6,000 e-scooters
questions.explanation: The regulation establishes a cap of 6,000 e-scooters per licence. This fleet limitation was intended to manage the shared mobility market, though the Council of State temporarily suspended these caps in April 2024 before they were later upheld.
questions.sourceTitle: Shared mobility in Brussels: e-scooters, bicycles, taxis and the battle over concessions

questions.question: What is the current status of the Villo! bicycle-sharing concession agreement with JCDecaux?
options: It has already been terminated and replaced by free-floating services only
It was renewed for another 10 years in 2024
It expires in September 2026 after nearly 17 years of operation
It was extended indefinitely with performance improvements required
questions.explanation: The Villo! concession agreement, signed in November 2008, ends in September 2026. The Brussels government has commissioned a comparative study but has not yet announced a decision on whether to maintain docking stations, transition to free-floating bikes, or adopt a hybrid model.
questions.sourceTitle: Shared mobility in Brussels: e-scooters, bicycles, taxis and the battle over concessions

questions.question: In Brussels, which authority is responsible for approving and funding Dutch-language nurseries?
options: Opgroeien (formerly Kind en Gezin)
Iriscare
ONE (Office de la Naissance et de l'Enfance)
The Brussels municipalities
questions.explanation: Opgroeien, representing the Flemish Community, handles approval and funding of Dutch-language nurseries in Brussels, while ONE manages francophone structures. This division of authority across language communities is a defining feature of Brussels governance.
questions.sourceTitle: Early childhood in Brussels: nurseries, waiting lists and triple institutional authority

questions.question: Why do separate waiting lists for francophone and Dutch-language nurseries create a practical problem for Brussels families?
options: They require families to speak both languages to apply
They are managed by the same authority, causing confusion
Families can wait months in one queue while a place opens in another queue they are not registered on
They prevent families from moving between communes
questions.explanation: Since waiting lists are kept separately by language community and institution, a family waiting for a francophone place has no access to an available Dutch-language place unless they have registered on that separate list. This structural fragmentation directly impacts families' ability to return to work after maternity leave.
questions.sourceTitle: Early childhood in Brussels: nurseries, waiting lists and triple institutional authority

questions.question: Why is TFA (trifluoroacetic acid) a particular challenge for Brussels drinking water suppliers compared to other PFAS contaminants?
options: It is produced by a major factory in Brussels, unlike other PFAS sources
Standard activated carbon filters cannot remove it, requiring more expensive treatment methods like reverse osmosis
It has been regulated in Brussels since 2024, making it illegal to distribute
It is only found in the Daussoulx-Boitsfort reservoir and nowhere else in the Region
questions.explanation: TFA's resistance to standard filtration methods creates a significant technical and cost problem for water treatment. This distinction is crucial for understanding why Brussels faces a unique drinking water challenge even though Vivaqua confirms other PFAS standards are met.
questions.sourceTitle: PFAS in Brussels: soil and water contamination

questions.question: What is the key difference between the health thresholds recommended by the Superior Health Council and the current legal drinking water standards in Brussels?
options: There is no difference; they use the same methodology and numerical values
The Superior Health Council only applies to Flanders, not to Brussels
The legal standards are based on precaution while the Superior Health Council uses industry data
The Superior Health Council recommends stricter thresholds based on the precautionary principle rather than just minimum quality requirements
questions.explanation: The Superior Health Council's 'maximum health values' represent a precautionary approach with much stricter limits than current legal 'quality limits.' Understanding this distinction helps explain why civil society organisations like Inter-Environnement Bruxelles call for regulatory reform based on public health rather than minimum compliance.
questions.sourceTitle: PFAS in Brussels: soil and water contamination

questions.question: According to the new Brussels security plan, which of the following is a newly created institutional role to address drug-related crime?
options: Federal Railway Police director
Regional anti-drugs commissioner
SNCB security coordinator
Bruxelles Prévention & Sécurité director
questions.explanation: The government agreement of 12 February 2026 introduced a dedicated regional anti-drugs commissioner as a new authority specifically focused on combating narcotics in Brussels. This represents a structural innovation in how the region coordinates its drug enforcement efforts.
questions.sourceTitle: Security plan: anti-drugs commissioner, 10M EUR for stations and police zone merger

questions.question: Under the Brussels administration reform announced in February 2026, which functions are being pooled together in the 'Shared support service' pillar?
options: Human resources, IT, finance, budget, and statistics
Public transport construction, parking management, and infrastructure maintenance
Land management, social housing coordination, and property development
Urban planning, housing, and environmental operations
questions.explanation: Pillar 1 consolidates cross-cutting administrative functions that support all regional entities. Understanding these transversal services is key to grasping how the reform aims to reduce duplication and improve efficiency across the Brussels administration.
questions.sourceTitle: Reform of the regional administration: from 25 entities to 4 pillars

questions.question: Which two Brussels institutions jointly lead the Shifting Economy strategy adopted in 2022?
options: hub.brussels and finance&invest.brussels
Brussels Economy and Employment (BEE) and Brupartners
Brupartners and Brussels Environment
Brussels Environment and Brussels Economy and Employment (BEE)
questions.explanation: The Shifting Economy is jointly led by Brussels Environment and Brussels Economy and Employment (BEE), with additional support from hub.brussels and finance&invest.brussels. Understanding the governance structure helps clarify how the Region coordinates its economic transition efforts.
questions.sourceTitle: Economic Transition: Shifting Economy, Labels and Sustainability Reporting

questions.question: What was the main shift in approach introduced by the new accreditation standards for nursing homes in Brussels, effective since September 2024?
options: From public sector management to mandatory private commercial operation
From unlimited accreditation periods to a maximum of 6 years per facility
From a care-centred model to a resident-centred model focusing on autonomy and quality of life
From individual care plans to standardized treatment protocols for all residents
questions.explanation: The 2024 accreditation standards represent a fundamental reform in how Brussels nursing homes must operate, prioritizing residents' autonomy and participation rather than purely medical care delivery. This reflects evolving best practices in elderly care governance.
questions.sourceTitle: Seniors in Brussels: housing, autonomy and data

questions.question: What was the main reason that major renovation and construction projects for Brussels social housing were frozen between June 2024 and February 2026?
options: A caretaker government period prevented new investment decisions from being made
The Brussels Region was implementing a new social housing allocation policy
The SLRB lacked sufficient funding to proceed with any projects
An audit of social housing providers revealed systemic governance failures
questions.explanation: The caretaker government period created a legal and political deadlock that prevented structural investment decisions. Once the new government agreement was signed on 12 February 2026, this blockage was lifted, unblocking multi-year renovation and construction plans.
questions.sourceTitle: SLRB: 1,000+ public housing units and financial consolidation

questions.question: Which of the following best describes how the SLRB plans to address its financial debt through asset disposals?
options: Converting SISP-managed buildings into private rental properties
Selling social housing units directly to tenants to generate immediate revenue
Leasing public assets to private developers for long-term income
Selling land sites and some social housing units to reduce debt to the Regional government
questions.explanation: The government decided to sell several assets including the Ariane and Palais land sites, plus approximately 200 social housing units. These disposals aim to reduce the SLRB's debt to the Brussels Region while a deconsolidation fund will help remove this debt from the regional budget.
questions.sourceTitle: SLRB: 1,000+ public housing units and financial consolidation

questions.question: Why does Brussels Airport's runway 25R primarily direct aircraft over densely populated areas of Brussels?
options: Because the Royal Castle exclusion zone forces all traffic through the city center
Because prevailing south-westerly winds require take-offs towards Brussels on this runway
Because European aviation regulations mandate this flight path
Because it is the longest runway and can handle the most traffic
questions.explanation: The structural geography of the airport means south-westerly winds (which occur about 70% of the time) naturally require aircraft to take off on runway 25R in a direction that overflies central and northern Brussels. This technical constraint has been at the core of the 40-year conflict between airport operations and residents' quality of life.
questions.sourceTitle: Brussels Overflight: 40 Years of Conflict, 450,000 Affected Residents

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the deconsolidation fund created by the Brussels government agreement of February 2026?
options: To cover the costs of sewer network renovations
To remove Vivaqua's debt from the regional budget
To collect unpaid water bills from households
To finance the integration of Hydria into Vivaqua
questions.explanation: The deconsolidation fund is a specific financial mechanism designed to separate Vivaqua's debt from the regional budget, allowing the Region to provide a recapitalisation while managing its fiscal position separately. This is a key structural innovation in the government agreement.
questions.sourceTitle: Vivaqua: financial consolidation announced
